Just now

--

How I used GPT-4.1, Claude, Qwen, and Band SDK to simulate a multidisciplinary cancer board with built-in safety checks
Most AI healthcare demos look impressive for a few minutes.
A model reads a patient case, generates a diagnosis, suggests a treatment plan, and everyone nods.
Real medicine doesn’t work like that.
In cancer care, treatment decisions are rarely made by a single expert. Instead, hospitals run Tumor Boards — multidisciplinary meetings where radiologists, pathologists, surgeons, and oncologists review a patient’s case together before deciding on the next steps.
These discussions can be incredibly valuable, but they also come with challenges:

• Coordinating multiple specialists takes time

• Clinical reasoning is often discussed verbally and never fully documented

• Minority opinions can disappear during discussion

• There is rarely a detailed audit trail showing how a decision was reached
That made me wonder:
Could a group of AI agents simulate the same collaborative decision-making process while keeping every opinion, disagreement, and safety concern fully traceable?
To explore that idea, I built an AI Tumor Board for the Band Hackathon.
The result was a multi-agent system where six AI participants collaborate inside a shared room, debate a cancer case, challenge each other’s assumptions, and produce an auditable board recommendation that still requires human review.

The Idea
Instead of asking one model to generate a treatment recommendation, I wanted to recreate the workflow used in real multidisciplinary teams.
Each specialist focuses exclusively on their own area of expertise.
🩻 Radiologist
Reviews imaging findings and determines clinical staging.
🔬 Pathologist
Evaluates histology, tumor grade, and biomarkers.
💊 Medical Oncologist
Assesses systemic treatment options.
🏥 Surgeon
Determines resectability and surgical strategy.
⚠️ Safety Officer
Reviews everything and can veto the entire recommendation if critical information is missing.
🎯 Coordinator
Manages the discussion and orchestrates the workflow.
The goal wasn’t replacing clinicians.
The goal was creating a structured reasoning process that humans could inspect, challenge, and approve.

Why I Chose Band SDK
The biggest challenge wasn’t generating medical opinions.
It was getting multiple agents to collaborate reliably.
I needed:

• Shared context

• Turn-based conversations

• Cross-agent communication

• Auditability

• Support for multiple AI frameworks
Band provided all of that through a room-based architecture.
The room itself became the tumor board.
Every participant could access the same patient data, publish structured opinions, and contribute to a single transcript that later became the final board report.
The transcript wasn’t just a communication channel.
It became the system’s source of truth.

Press enter or click to view image in full size

The Band room transcript showing agent deliberation

Architecture Overview
One of the most interesting parts of this project was that the agents didn’t all run on the same stack.
AgentModelFrameworkRadiologistGPT-4.1PydanticAIPathologistClaude SonnetClaude SDKMedical OncologistQwen 3 32BPydanticAISurgeonGPT-4.1PydanticAISafety OfficerGPT-4.1CodexCoordinatorScript DrivenCustom
This wasn’t done simply to satisfy a hackathon requirement.
It was a deliberate design choice.
The Safety Officer runs on a different framework than several of the specialists it evaluates.
If one model family develops a systematic blind spot, an independent reviewer running on another stack has a better chance of catching it.

The Engineering Challenges Nobody Warned Me About
Building the agents was actually the easy part.
Making them behave consistently was much harder.
1. Structured Output Is Harder Than It Looks
Every specialist had to return machine-readable JSON.
GPT-4.1 followed the format almost perfectly.
Claude was reliable too.
Qwen occasionally decided that a beautifully written explanation was more important than the schema I requested.
The solution was a simple nudge-and-retry mechanism.
If the required structure was missing, the system asked the agent to respond again using only the expected format.
That single change dramatically improved reliability.
2. LLMs Are Terrible Meeting Coordinators
My first version used an LLM-powered coordinator.
Huge mistake.
The coordinator kept participating in discussions instead of simply managing them.
That triggered specialists to respond again, which triggered more responses, creating conversational loops.
Eventually, I removed the coordinator model entirely.
Now orchestration is handled by deterministic code while LLMs focus exclusively on reasoning.
The result is significantly more stable.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Each specialist is invited, responds, and is removed before the next3. One Broken Markdown Block Can Break Everything
Several models occasionally returned incomplete code blocks.
One missing triple backtick was enough to break downstream parsing and corrupt the final recommendation.
The fix was surprisingly simple:
Instead of parsing the entire transcript, I parsed each message independently.
A malformed response could no longer affect the rest of the conversation.

Why the Safety Officer Became My Favorite Agent
Initially, the Safety Officer existed mainly because I thought judges would appreciate the concept.
Then I started testing realistic synthetic cases.
The Safety Officer consistently identified issues such as:

• Missing biomarker results

• Incomplete staging information

• Conflicting specialist conclusions

• Recommendations based on unavailable evidence
What surprised me most was how often the safest recommendation was:
“Stop. A human needs to review this.”
That is exactly the behavior I wanted.
Not confidence.
Not certainty.
Appropriate escalation.

What the Output Looks Like
After deliberation, the system generates a structured tumor board report containing:

• Patient summary

• Specialist opinions

• Areas of agreement

• Areas of disagreement

• Safety review

• Consensus recommendation

• Human sign-off requirement
Every recommendation explicitly includes:
{
"requires_human_review": true
}No autonomous clinical decisions.
No hidden reasoning.
Everything remains reviewable and traceable.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The exported board note with all specialist opinions and safety verdict.Example Recommendation
Case: Triple-negative breast cancer, Stage IIB
Radiologist: cT2N1M0 with suspicious axillary lymph nodes.
Pathologist: Grade 3 invasive ductal carcinoma with high Ki-67. Recommend BRCA testing.
Medical Oncologist: Neoadjuvant AC-T with pembrolizumab.
Surgeon: Breast-conserving surgery if response is adequate.
Safety Officer: Clear to proceed but BRCA status should be obtained before finalizing adjuvant treatment.
Consensus: Neoadjuvant chemo-immunotherapy followed by surgery, pending biomarker confirmation and human review.

Lessons Learned
Multi-agent systems are fundamentally different from AI chains
Independent specialists create richer reasoning than a single sequential workflow.
Deterministic orchestration beats AI orchestration
Models should contribute expertise, not manage the process.
Diversity improves safety
Running reviewers on different frameworks and providers creates useful redundancy.
Auditability matters
The conversation itself becomes part of the product.
Safety mechanisms aren’t optional
Sometimes the most valuable recommendation is simply:
“We need more information before proceeding.”

Final Thoughts
Building this project changed how I think about AI systems.
The most interesting future isn’t a single super-intelligent model making decisions alone.
It’s specialized agents collaborating, disagreeing, validating each other’s work, and producing outputs that humans can trust because every step is visible.
That’s what I wanted to explore with the AI Tumor Board.
And this feels like just the beginning.
